ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a Spanish mackerel food product entry; steamed preparation variant is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated steamed Spanish mackerel code; A00GJ (Spanish mackerel, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for steamed Spanish mackerel; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of steamed Spanish mackerel is approximately 120 g, equivalent to one medium boneless fillet portion as commonly served in Singaporean home meals or hawker settings.

nutrient_source.title: Spanish Mackerel (Batang), Steamed — Singapore Health Promotion Board Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for steamed Spanish mackerel (batang). All core macros and micronutrients reflect the HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at 1.8 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central NDB 15040 (Spanish mackerel, cooked, dry heat) fatty acid profile proportionally applied to HPB total fat of 7.5 g/100 g; documented in notes.

gi_source.title: N/A — Negligible carbohydrate content; GI not applicable for pure protein-fat foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Steamed Spanish mackerel contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database does not test foods with negligible carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (60 mg/100 g) with high potassium (451 mg/100 g) yielding a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary guidelines
hypertension.guidance: Steamed Spanish mackerel is well-suited for hypertensive patients due to its low sodium content of 60 mg/100 g and high potassium of 451 mg/100 g; patients should avoid adding soy sauce or salted condiments to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content produce no glycaemic load; high protein (23 g/100 g) supports postprandial glucose stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: With zero carbohydrates and zero sugar, steamed Spanish mackerel does not raise blood glucose and is an excellent protein source for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; patients should avoid sweet sauces or marinades that would add carbohydrate to this otherwise glycaemically neutral dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) with low saturated fat (~1.8 g/100 g) and no trans fat, supporting endothelial function and reducing triglycerides
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed Spanish mackerel provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids that reduce triglycerides and support endothelial health; steaming preserves omega-3 integrity and avoids the pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products generated by frying, making it a preferred preparation for cardiovascular disease patients.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High omega-3 (EPA/DHA) content suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokines and reduces joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Spanish mackerel is a rich source of EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids that directly attenuate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; steaming is the preferred preparation as it preserves omega-3 integrity, whereas frying generates AGEs and degrades beneficial fatty acids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Spanish mackerel (batang) is a minimally processed fish dish consisting of boneless fillets of Scomberomorus commerson — locally known as batang or tenggiri — cooked by steaming without added fat or seasoning. The flesh is white to light grey, moist, and flaky with a mild, clean marine flavour. Steaming preserves the fish's natural omega-3 fatty acid profile (EPA and DHA), which is clinically significant for cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits. Per 100 g, the dish provides 160 kcal, 23 g protein, 7.5 g fat (approximately 1.8 g saturated), and zero carbohydrates, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. Sodium is very low at 60 mg/100 g while potassium is high at 451 mg/100 g, an excellent ratio for hypertension management consistent with DASH guidelines. Cholesterol is 90 mg/100 g, which is moderate and offset by the cardioprotective fatty acid profile. In Singapore, steamed batang is commonly served as a main protein dish in home cooking and hawker settings, often accompanied by steamed rice and vegetables. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. The dish is nutritionally dense, high in protein, and low in sodium, making it one of the most clinically recommended fish preparations for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: Is batang fish good for diabetics?
Is steamed mackerel good for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database at confidence 0.95 for an exact match to steamed Spanish mackerel (batang). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 15040 proportional fatty acid profile applied to HPB total fat; this single adaptation does not reduce overall confidence below high given the primary source quality.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as Spanish mackerel is a pure animal protein and fat source containing no carbohydrate by composition; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as fish contains no plant fibre. Trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible in lean steamed fish per USDA FoodData Central NDB 15040. Saturated_fat_g of 1.8 g/100 g is estimated by applying the USDA FoodData Central NDB 15040 (Spanish mackerel, cooked, dry heat) fatty acid proportion (~24% of total fat as saturated) to the HPB-reported total fat of 7.5 g/100 g; this is an adaptation and is documented accordingly. The following fields are set to -1 because detailed data is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry used, and USDA FoodData Central NDB 15040 was not used as primary source for these fields to avoid source mixing without full reconciliation: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid breakdown for steamed batang not reported in HPB database; water_g — moisture content not reported in HPB database entry for this preparation; vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg — full vitamin panel not reported in HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for this steamed fish entry; phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg — these mineral fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for steamed Spanish mackerel (batang). Source consulted and confirmed unavailable: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (foodo.hpb.gov.sg), accessed 2026-04-16. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); glycemic index set to 0 as numeric sentinel per template requirement with gi_category not_applicable; glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03411433 is the closest defensible match for Spanish mackerel food product; steamed preparation is not separately coded. FoodEx2 code A00GJ is used as the closest defensible cooked Spanish mackerel anchor.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English food name present. Singaporean dish: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names all populated. Food identity is precise and unambiguous.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (160), protein_g (23), fat_g (7.5), carbohydrate_g (0) all present and numeric. Source is HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (exact match, confidence 0.95). Zero carbohydrate justified in notes as naturally zero.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0 — naturally zero, justified), sodium_mg (60.0), saturated_fat_g (1.8 — estimated from USDA NDB 15040 proportional adaptation, documented), sugar_g (0.0 — naturally zero, justified). All four required secondary fields present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to 0 as numeric value per template requirement; gi_category set to not_applicable for negligible carbohydrate food. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source documents the rationale referencing University of Sydney GI Database standard. Notes field clarifies GI is not applicable as food contains negligible carbohydrates.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03411433 used as closest_parent defensible match with gap note. FoodEx2 A00GJ (Spanish mackerel, cooked) used as closest_parent defensible anchor with gap note. SNOMED CT null with honest gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers food identity, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrient profile. Common user queries and meal context populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern), Type 2 Diabetes (human_condition_name correct), Cardiovascular Disease (low concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid arthritis included as additional condition.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Flat aliases array contains 13 entries covering English variants, Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay variants, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized forms. aliases_by_language is fully populated and consistent with flat aliases. Self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language values appear verbatim in flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced from input JSON.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types to closest_parent; documented -1 values in notes; confirmed glycemic_index null for negligible carbohydrate food.

Iteration 3: Fixed G3 hard gate failure by adding comprehensive documentation of all -1 fields in notes, explicitly naming each field, stating the reason data is unavailable, and citing the source consulted (HPB Singapore Food Composition Database). Fixed Q4 failure by setting glycemic_index to numeric value 0 (template requires numeric) with gi_category not_applicable and full explanatory notes clarifying the not-applicable rationale.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all hard gates and scores 8/8. G3 failure from iteration 2 resolved by fully documenting all 17 fields set to -1 in the notes field with field names, unavailability reason, and source consulted. Q4 failure resolved by setting glycemic_index to numeric 0 with gi_category not_applicable and explanatory notes. All aliases_by_language values verified verbatim in flat aliases array. All mandatory Singaporean multilingual names present.
